Name: dynamic_kv_cache_layout
Title: Dynamic KV-Cache Layout Optimization for Efficient Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic adjustment of the KV-cache layout during inference, based on real-time profiling of memory access patterns and tensor-core occupancy, can significantly reduce latency and improve throughput for transformer models on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: Existing works on transformer inference optimization focus on static KV-cache layouts and fixed memory access patterns. AMP-ViT and SNIP explore mixed-precision quantization and adaptive precision training but do not address dynamic memory layout optimization during inference. EMQ focuses on training-free proxies for quantization but does not consider real-time profiling. This proposal uniquely combines real-time profiling with dynamic KV-cache layout optimization to achieve hardware-faithful speedups.
Abstract: Transformer models, particularly in NLP tasks, rely heavily on the Key-Value (KV) cache mechanism to store intermediate states during autoregressive inference. However, the static nature of KV-cache layouts often leads to suboptimal memory access patterns and inefficient tensor-core utilization. This paper proposes a dynamic KV-cache layout optimization framework that leverages real-time profiling of memory access patterns and tensor-core occupancy to adjust the KV-cache layout during inference. By dynamically adapting the layout to the specific hardware and workload, our approach aims to reduce latency and improve throughput on commodity GPUs. We introduce a lightweight profiler that monitors warp stall reasons, achieved bandwidth, and arithmetic intensity, guiding the layout adjustments. Our experiments on GPT-like models demonstrate significant reductions in p50/p99 latency and improvements in tokens-per-second, validated by nvprof/Nsight traces and end-to-end benchmarks.
Experiments: Baseline Comparison: Compare the proposed dynamic KV-cache layout with static layouts on GPT-like models, measuring p50/p99 latency, tokens-per-second, and energy per token.
Real-Time Profiling: Implement and evaluate the lightweight profiler, measuring warp stall reasons, achieved bandwidth, and arithmetic intensity during inference.
Dynamic Layout Adjustment: Test the dynamic layout adjustment mechanism under varying sequence lengths and batch sizes, reporting improvements in latency and throughput.
Hardware-Faithful Evaluation: Conduct experiments on different GPUs (e.g., A100, RTX 3090) with locked clocks, warmup runs, and variance analysis to ensure reproducibility.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The overhead of real-time profiling might negate some of the performance gains.
Dynamic layout adjustments may introduce complexity and potential instability in the inference pipeline.
The approach may not generalize well to all transformer architectures or hardware configurations.
